MBS holdings by commercial banks face a structural headwind: banks are reluctant to actively reinvest in MBS due to previous unrealized losses from rate hikes and are instead allowing their portfolios to organically run off as underlying mortgages amortize or are paid off. Although mark-to-market (MTM) fluctuations from interest rate changes can cause brief increases in the dollar value of Available-for-Sale (AFS) portfolios, the overarching trend consists of declining principal balances due to limited new bank demand. Given this fundamental drag, the likelihood of an aggregate increase in MBS holdings is below 50% across the evaluated horizons. Following the structural requirement, probabilities drift closer to 0.5 for more distant dates, reflecting increased uncertainty over the trajectory of interest rates and bank asset allocation in longer horizons.

To reach 15 bcm in any quarter, the EU would require a massive surge in both pipeline and LNG imports that physical capacity and policy largely prohibit. In Q1 2024, with the Ukraine transit route fully operational, imports were less than 14 bcm. Following the end of the Ukraine transit route in Jan 2025, the EU lost roughly 3.5-4 bcm/quarter in pipeline capacity. The maximum run rates in February 2025 (56 mcm/d for TurkStream, 74.3 mcm/d for LNG) equates to approximately 11.7 bcm per quarter. Even if 100% of Russia's remaining unsanctioned European-facing LNG (from Yamal) was diverted from Asia to the EU, it would only add about 1 bcm/quarter, capping maximal physical capacity around 12.5-13 bcm/quarter. Reaching 15 bcm would necessitate an unexpected resumption of the Ukraine transit pipeline running at peak historical rates simultaneously with maximum TurkStream and LNG volumes. Given the EU plan to phase out Russian gas by 2027 and these physical constraints, a 15 bcm quarter is effectively impossible. I am inputting 0.05 according to boundary constraints.

During developing La Niña years, anomalies usually remain flat or decrease into autumn, making a bounce back up to 1.10°C (which requires a pre-industrial equivalent of ~1.38°C) very unlikely. Small uncertainties remain regarding month-to-month volatility (weather noise) or underlying permanent shifts from reduced aerosol cooling, but the evidence strongly suggests October 2025 will be below the 1.10°C threshold.
